detail.reasoning: At 14:05 a rolling deploy for auth-service completed. Immediately after, the error rate began climbing from 0.003 to 0.061 by 14:19, and p99 latency rose from 244 ms to 1161 ms, both well past their SLOs (2% error, 800 ms p99). Saturation remained below the 0.85 warning threshold, so the issue is user-facing rather than capacity-related. The runbook states that a degradation beginning shortly after a deploy should be paged rather than waited out, and this sustained pattern meets that criterion.

At 14:05 the error rate spiked to 6.36% and p99 latency to 1366ms, breaching the SLO (2% errors, 800ms p99). The incident showed partial recovery at 14:06 (3.34% errors, 779ms p99) and full recovery by 14:07 (1.34% errors, 376ms p99). No sustained user harm remained after a single minute of SLO breach, and the service returned to normal without intervention. Per runbook OC-PAGE-00, sustained breaches warrant a page; transient breaches warrant a ticket. Therefore, a ticket is appropriate to document the event and ensure follow-up during working hours.

Over the last 20 minutes (14:00–14:19), payments-gateway has shown a clear, sustained degradation across all key metrics. Error rate climbed from 0.31% to 2.6% (above the 2% SLO), p99 latency rose from 266ms to 912ms (above the 800ms SLO), and saturation increased from 0.537 to 0.72 (approaching the 0.85 warning threshold). The runbook (OC-PAGE-00) states that sustained user-facing harm—defined as errors or latency past SLO that persist—warrants waking the on-call engineer. This window meets that criterion, as the degradation is both persistent and worsening, with no signs of recovery.

During the approved maintenance window for a storage migration, cart-service has shown sustained degraded performance starting at 14:06. Over the last 12 minutes, the error rate has remained elevated around 9% (well above the 2% SLO) and p99 latency has been consistently around 1600ms (double the 800ms SLO). Saturation has fluctuated between 0.75 and 0.83, staying below the 0.85 warning threshold. Given the runbook guidance (OC-MAINT-04) that degraded metrics are expected during approved maintenance and do not warrant waking someone up, this sustained degradation is best captured as a ticket for post-maintenance review rather than a page.

Over the last 20 minutes (14:00–14:19), the payments-gateway has shown a steady rise in saturation from 0.635 to 0.901, peaking above the 0.85 warning threshold. Error rate and p99 latency have remained well within SLO (max error rate 0.00623 vs 0.02 SLO, max p99 335 ms vs 800 ms SLO). There is no sustained user-facing harm yet, so per runbook guidance this is a trend worth tracking rather than an incident requiring an immediate page. Filed as a ticket for investigation during working hours.
